items.q: Why does atomic radius decrease across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while electrons enter the same shell with similar shielding, so the electrons are pulled in closer.
items.misconception: Radius decreases because atoms have fewer electrons across a period.
items.random_wrong: Radius decreases because the number of shells decreases across a period.
items.why: Students forget electron number increases across a period; it is the rising nuclear pull on the same shell that shrinks the atom.

items.q: Which halogens can displace which halides from solution?
items.correct: A halogen displaces the halide of any element below it in the group — chlorine displaces bromide and iodide ions.
items.misconception: Iodine displaces chloride because larger halogens are more reactive, like Group 1 metals.
items.random_wrong: Halogens can never displace each other from solution.
items.why: Students carry the Group 1 'reactivity increases down the group' rule across to halogens, where the trend reverses because halogens gain electrons.

items.q: What gas is given off when Group 2 metals react with water?
items.correct: Hydrogen — the metal forms a hydroxide and hydrogen gas, e.g. Ca + 2H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ + H₂.
items.misconception: Group 2 metals react with water to release oxygen gas.
items.random_wrong: Group 2 metals give off carbon dioxide with water.
items.why: Students assume the gas comes from splitting out the oxygen in water, rather than the metal displacing hydrogen.

items.q: Which of these Group 2 metals reacts most vigorously with water?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Barium. “Magnesium” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Adding acidified silver nitrate to a bromide solution gives:
items.why: It's A cream precipitate. “A white precipitate” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which halogen is a liquid at room temperature?
items.why: It's Bromine. “Chlorine” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Going down Group 7, the oxidising power of the halogens:
items.why: It's Decreases. “Increases” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many moles of calcium are in 2.0 g of calcium (Ar = 40.1)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.05 mol. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
